change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew from 123 to 1154 (+838%); tldr_evidence (278 chars) and tldr_verdict (107 chars) added since c2; sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-05T20:52Z. New content is the templated post-hoc 'trivial-transcription test' paragraph in detail_verdict_text plus two new TLDRs.
our_argument_summary: O1 (25.82 h) is the principal lunar diurnal constituent — period derived from Doodson 1921. The dome adopts the standard period without deriving it from disc geometry, and its local moon at 2,534 km cannot replicate the global diurnal modulation observed across all ocean basins. Verdict: Self-Contradicted (correct).

relevant_files: raw-text/08-live-power.txt — Live Model Power dashboard rollup that mirrors monitor.py output
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: raw-text/08-live-power.txt lines 212-244 list M2, K1, S2 tidal domains in the live dashboard with explicit predicted/observed/tolerance/source. O1 and N2 are absent from that list. Section 'TAUTOLOGICAL DOMAINS' (line 297) explicitly enumerates 'M2=12.42h, K1=23.9345h, S2=12.0h' — O1/N2 are not part of the verification suite.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no monitoring code exists for O1 in any form (hardcoded, live-fetch, or otherwise).
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None; no monitoring code at all.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The new detail_verdict_text claim 'no monitor.py entry exists for this tidal constituent at all' is corroborated by raw-text/08-live-power.txt (M2/K1/S2 listed in 'Live Model Power' rollup; O1 absent). c2 already verified this; c3 re-confirms it via independent lookup.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/08-live-power.txt lists the dashboard's tidal domains explicitly: 'Domain: M2 Tidal Period' (line 212), 'Domain: K1 Tidal Period' (line 216), 'Domain: S2 Solar Tidal Period' (line 240). No O1 domain appears. The 'Tautological Domains' enumeration on line 297 also omits O1.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: O1 is a textbook constituent of the Doodson (1921) harmonic expansion, derived from gravitational forcing by a distant moon and sun. The dome adopts the standard 25.82 h value verbatim; no alternative numerical prediction is offered. The aetheric mechanism is a renaming, not a new computation.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Doodson 1921 catalogued O1's period (25.819 h) over a century before the dome model existed. The dome's V51.0 'WIN' is the published value re-stated; no derivation, timestamp, or pre-data prediction document exists for it.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No derivation in raw-text or on the dome site connects O1 to dome parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, sun_altitude=5,733 km, moon_altitude=2,534 km, H(r)). The period is stated as matching observation, not calculated from disc geometry.

holes_found.description: TLDR factual error — diurnal/semidiurnal conflation propagated into BOTH new TLDRs. tldr_evidence: 'The dome's nearby moon at 2,534 km would produce one sharp local tidal pulse, not the observed global two-bulge pattern.' tldr_verdict: 'The dome's local moon can't produce the two-bulge pattern actually observed globally.' Per the curmudgeon prompt's TLDR rule, factual errors at the TLDR level are MAJOR because readers see TLDRs first and may not expand. The 'two-bulge pattern' is the M2/S2 (semidiurnal) signature — TWO high tides per day from two opposing bulges. O1 is DIURNAL — ONE high tide per day, generated by lunar declination making successive semidiurnal high tides unequal (the diurnal inequality), NOT by a 'two-bulge' mechanism. A tidal-literate dome defender will pull a single screenshot of these TLDRs and argue 'their own debunk doesn't understand the difference between diurnal and semidiurnal tides.' WIN-049 (K1, the analogous luni-solar diurnal) was already corrected in c2 to use 'localized tidal spike, not the observed global pattern' (no 'two-bulge' framing). WIN-050 needs the same treatment.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite both TLDRs to drop the 'two-bulge' framing. Mirror WIN-049's TLDR style: tldr_evidence — 'O1 (25.82 hours) is another standard tidal constituent from the Doodson 1921 expansion. The dome's nearby moon at 2,534 km would produce a sharp localized tidal pulse, not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed globally.' tldr_verdict — 'Tabulated since 1921. The dome's nearby moon can't produce the observed global diurnal pattern.' These are TLDR-shaped (≤ 3 sentences) and physically correct for a diurnal constituent.

holes_found.description: Same diurnal/semidiurnal conflation now also appears in detail_verdict_text (NEW with the c3 expansion): 'Dome's local moon at 2,534 km produces one sharp tidal pulse, not the observed two-bulge global pattern.' This is the 'verdict' summary block readers see when they expand the panel. Combined with the unfixed detail_evidence sentence 'The globe's distant moon produces two nearly symmetric bulges (near-side and far-side) producing the observed once-per-lunar-day diurnal tidal pattern' — that detail_evidence sentence is internally incoherent: two bulges produce SEMIdiurnal forcing (twice per day), not 'once-per-lunar-day diurnal.' Two bulges plus rotation = M2-type pattern, not O1. The c2 review flagged this same incoherence in detail_evidence as moderate; the c3 expansion has now propagated the wrong framing into detail_verdict_text without fixing the source.
holes_found.recommendation: (a) In detail_verdict_text, replace 'not the observed two-bulge global pattern' with 'not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation observed globally.' (b) In detail_evidence, fix the c2-flagged sentence: replace 'The globe's distant moon produces two nearly symmetric bulges (near-side and far-side) producing the observed once-per-lunar-day diurnal tidal pattern across all ocean basins simultaneously' with the WIN-049-style framing: 'O1 arises from the moon's declination ≈ ±28.6° relative to the equatorial plane producing once-per-day inequality between successive semidiurnal high tides — a smooth latitude-dependent modulation requiring a distant moon (d/R >> 1).' This makes both fields physically correct AND aligns the K1/O1 pair on a single mechanism story.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN numerical inconsistency (Lens B). WIN-050 detail_evidence: 'tidal force is a localized spike — dropping to 9% of peak at 5,000 km offset and 0.6% at the equator.' WIN-049 detail_evidence: 'tidal force drops to 9% at 5,000 km offset and 1.48% at the equator.' The 9%-at-5,000-km figure agrees in both (verified: (2534/sqrt(5000²+2534²))³ = 9.24%). But the 'equator' percentages disagree by ~2.5×. Computing 1/r³ falloff from a moon at 2,534 km altitude over a 20,015-km-radius disc with the equator at radial distance ~10,000 km from the pole gives 1.48% — matching WIN-049, not WIN-050. The WIN-050 figure of 0.6% only reproduces if 'equator' is interpreted as ~13,700 km from sub-lunar (which doesn't match the dome's standard equator radius). A defender comparing the two WINs will spot the discrepancy and challenge our arithmetic in BOTH WINs. Either WIN-050's 0.6% is a transcription error (should be 1.48%), or both WINs need to clarify exactly which disc-radial point they mean by 'equator.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace WIN-050's '0.6% at the equator' with '1.48% at the equator,' matching WIN-049 and the canonical 1/r³ falloff calculation: ratio = (h/d)³ where d = sqrt(R²+h²), h=2,534 km, R=10,000 km → 1.48%. If a different geographic point is intended, name it explicitly (e.g., 'at the disc rim, 20,015 km from pole, the falloff reaches 0.20%'). One number per geographic point, used identically across all five tidal WINs.

holes_found.description: Section anchor and tab-switch click-handler in detail_evidence: 'Section 2.2'. The href '#part2' is the tab anchor (per V6 restructure: Part 4.5 → Part 2 → tab id 'selftest'), and Section 2.2 ('Tidal Pattern: One Spike vs. Two Bulges') exists in sections.json — verified. So the link resolves. However, this is a verbatim repeat of the WIN-049 link target, which is appropriate for a within-group cross-reference. No issue, just confirming the V6 renaming has been applied consistently across the tidal group.
holes_found.recommendation: No change required. Noted as a positive consistency check.

holes_found.description: Finding text says 'concentrates force at one point.' Per the c2 review, this overstates localization (the moon at 2,534 km produces a force CONE roughly 5,000 km wide at 9% intensity, not literally a point). The c2 review marked this minor and recommended 'in a localized cone beneath the sub-lunar track.' That recommendation has not been applied. Cosmetic, but if the major-severity rewrites above are picked up, this is a one-line tightening worth doing in the same patch.
holes_found.recommendation: Change finding to: 'Period is trivial (lunar diurnal); dome's local moon at 2,534 km concentrates force in a localized cone beneath the sub-lunar track, not the smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation required for O1.'

stronger_arguments.description: K1 + O1 sum-frequency fingerprint: 1/K1 + 1/O1 = 1/23.9345 + 1/25.8193 ≈ 0.04178 + 0.03873 = 0.08051 cycles/h, period ≈ 12.42 h, which is M2 — the principal lunar SEMIdiurnal. K1 and O1 are not independent constituents; they are the diurnal-decomposition partners whose sum reproduces M2. This is a direct fingerprint of orbital mechanics with a distant moon: M2 splits into K1 and O1 through the moon's declination cycle. The dome's local moon at 2,534 km has no orbital declination cycle — its moon merely circles the disc — yet O1, K1, AND M2 all appear at the right frequencies. Claiming all three independently is double-counting a single celestial-mechanics signature. We make this argument implicitly in Section 2.2 but not in WIN-050 itself; surfacing it would make the 'self-contradicted' verdict harder to dispute.

Latitude-dependent diurnal inequality: O1's amplitude at any port is a known function of geographic latitude (peaks near ±30° latitude, vanishes at the equator and poles by symmetry). This pattern is a direct fingerprint of the moon's declination relative to a SPHERICAL Earth's rotation axis. On a flat disc with a moon circling at fixed altitude, no analogous symmetry argument generates this latitude profile. The dome would need to derive the latitude-dependent amplitude curve from disc geometry alone — and demonstrably hasn't.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies O1 as a real, discrete tidal frequency in the 25.82-hour band (data/wins.json:050.detail_evidence). Recognizing harmonic structure beyond the popular 'twice-a-day tides' framing demonstrates the author has at least encountered Doodson-style harmonic tidal analysis (raw-text/08-live-power.txt:212-244 lists M2/K1/S2 in the live dashboard, indicating the author has decomposed tides harmonically before).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Naming the period is bibliography, not prediction. The dome offers no derivation linking O1's 25.82 h to disc parameters (disc_radius=20,015 km, moon_altitude=2,534 km, H(r)) — see code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence above. The dome's automation does not even include O1 in the live-power dashboard (raw-text/08-live-power.txt; dashboard lists M2/K1/S2 only). Adopting Doodson's 1921 catalogue and re-claiming the entries as confirmed predictions fails the dome author's own definition (https://john09289.github.io/predictions: 'a number derived from the model, registered with a timestamp, and subsequently confirmed by independent data') because the value was published a century before the dome existed.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender, granted our six methodological principles, mounts the following rebuttal. (1) On the 'two-bulge' wording in our TLDRs: 'The reviewers have confused themselves — they label O1 as diurnal, then attack the dome with a two-bulge image that everyone knows describes M2. Either the diurnal mechanism is the right frame, in which case the dome only has to produce a once-a-day forcing (which it does — the moon circles once per lunar day), or the two-bulge mechanism is the right frame, in which case the criticism applies to M2/S2 (which the dome already explains). The reviewers cannot have it both ways.' (2) On 'no monitor.py entry': 'The dashboard tracks the principal constituents M2, K1, S2. O1 and N2 are derivative — the same Doodson decomposition produces them automatically once the principal pair is fixed. Demanding a separate monitor.py entry for every harmonic is a category error.' (3) On 'no derivation from dome geometry': 'Standard tidal theory derives all 389 Doodson constituents from a single geometric set-up. The dome inherits the same harmonic decomposition once you accept aetheric forcing produces tidal stress at the moon's orbital frequency.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Defense (1) is the live wire — it directly exploits our two-bulge propagation and survives the four-out-of-five rating because it is rhetorically clean and our text genuinely is internally incoherent in the cited fields. Fixing the two TLDRs and the detail_verdict_text to use 'smooth latitude-dependent diurnal modulation' (per the major-hole recommendations above) closes (1). To pre-empt (2), add one sentence to detail_verdict_text after the 'no monitor.py entry' clause: 'The standard Doodson decomposition does generate O1 automatically once the principal lunar period is fixed — but the dome cannot inherit that derivation, since standard Doodson presupposes a distant gravitating moon, exactly the geometry the dome rejects.' To pre-empt (3), the existing 'No legitimate-novelty carve-out applies... the derivation (where one is offered at all) is algebraically equivalent to a standard-model derivation' clause already does the work; no further change needed. Net result: one targeted TLDR rewrite + one detail-evidence rewrite + one one-sentence preemptive insertion in detail_verdict_text = the strongest version of the dome defense collapses.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Three cross-WIN inconsistencies in the tidal group: (1) Diurnal/semidiurnal framing — WIN-049 (K1 diurnal) correctly uses 'localized tidal spike, not the observed global pattern' with no 'two-bulge' wording; WIN-050 (O1 diurnal) uses 'two-bulge global pattern' across detail_verdict_text + tldr_evidence + tldr_verdict. The two analogous diurnal WINs should describe the failure mode identically; they don't. (2) Equator percentage falloff — WIN-049 says 1.48%, WIN-050 says 0.6%, both attributing the value to 1/r³ falloff at 'the equator' on the dome. Computing the falloff from h=2,534 km, R=10,000 km gives 1.48%; 0.6% requires R≈13,700 km, which is not a standard 'equator' interpretation on the dome. (3) Five-tidal-WIN post-hoc paragraph templating: the five WINs split into two sub-templates — M2/K1/S2 use 'both predicted value (...) and observed value (...) static constants' (because monitor.py has these entries), while O1/N2 use 'no monitor.py entry exists for this tidal constituent at all.' This split is correct and matches the dashboard (raw-text/08-live-power.txt:212-244). The split is the kind of asymmetry that needs an explicit narrator note; otherwise a reader skimming might wonder whether the inconsistency is intentional.

claims_checked: O1 period = 25.82 h (claimed in tldr_evidence + detail_evidence)
Tidal force drops to 9% at 5,000 km offset (claimed in detail_evidence)
Tidal force drops to 0.6% at the equator (claimed in detail_evidence)
Doodson expansion contains 389 constituents (claimed in detail_evidence)
The numerical equality of WIN-049's 1.48%-at-equator and WIN-050's 0.6%-at-equator (cross-WIN)
quantitative_verification.computations: O1: angular speed = 13.943°/h (Doodson 1921), period = 360/13.943 = 25.8194 h, rounded 25.82 h — match ✓. Tidal-force ratio formula: (h/d)³ where d = sqrt(R² + h²), h = moon altitude 2,534 km, R = horizontal offset from sub-lunar point. At R = 5,000 km: d = sqrt(5000² + 2534²) = 5,605 km; ratio = (2534/5605)³ = 9.24% — claimed 9%, match ✓ (and matches WIN-049's 9% claim ✓). At R = 10,000 km (standard 'equator' on a 20,015-km-radius disc with sub-lunar point at the pole): d = 10,316 km; ratio = (2534/10316)³ = 1.482% — matches WIN-049's '1.48% at the equator' ✓, contradicts WIN-050's '0.6% at the equator' ✗. Inverting WIN-050: 0.6% requires R ≈ 13,687 km, which is not a natural interpretation of 'equator' on the dome (south rim at R = 20,015 km gives 0.20%). Doodson constituent count: Doodson 1921 catalogued ~386-396 constituents in the original paper (sources differ slightly); '389' is within the canonical range and consistent with the c2-approved finding.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: WIN-050 detail_evidence value '0.6% at the equator' does not reproduce from the standard 1/r³ falloff at the dome's equator-radius. The same calculation in WIN-049 (1.48%) does reproduce. WIN-050 should be corrected to 1.48% to match WIN-049 and the verified geometry, or specify a different geographic reference point and name it explicitly.

deeper_analysis: Cycle 3 trigger: drift-audit flagged WIN-050 as the top large-rewrite candidate (max_drift=838% on detail_verdict_length). The new content under review is the templated 'trivial-transcription test' post-hoc paragraph plus the two new TLDRs. The post-hoc paragraph is well-constructed and consistent with the rest of the tidal group (WIN-051 N2 uses the same 'no monitor.py entry exists' branch; WIN-045 M2 / WIN-046 S2 / WIN-049 K1 use the 'static constants' branch — split is correct given the actual monitor.py / dashboard contents in raw-text/08-live-power.txt:212-244). Verdict (Self-Contradicted) remains correct on independent grounds. The c3 expansion did NOT, however, fix the c2-flagged moderate hole (two-bulge framing in detail_evidence), and instead PROPAGATED that wrong framing into THREE additional fields: detail_verdict_text, tldr_evidence, tldr_verdict. Per the curmudgeon prompt's explicit TLDR rule ('TLDR factual errors are major because readers see TLDRs first and may not expand'), this elevates the c2 moderate to a c3 major. WIN-049 (the analogous luni-solar diurnal) was correctly fixed in c2 to use 'localized tidal spike' framing — WIN-050 needs the same coordinated fix across all four user-visible text fields. Independently, the cross-WIN consistency lens surfaced a 0.6%-vs-1.48% equator-percentage discrepancy between WIN-050 and WIN-049 that the c2 review did not catch. Verifying via 1/r³ tidal falloff (h=2,534 km, R=10,000 km → 1.48%) shows WIN-049 is correct and WIN-050's 0.6% is the outlier. Net recommendation: minor_edit to coordinate-fix the four diurnal/semidiurnal text fields plus the equator percentage. The verdict change column stays 'Self-Contradicted' — the underlying physics critique is unaffected.
